metadata.category: Number Theory | Mo's Algorithm | Offline Queries

content.problemStatement:
Read problems statements in Russian.You are given an array AAA of NNN positive integers. You are allowed to perform any of the following operations any number of times:


Pick an index iii (1≤i≤n1 \le i \le n1≤i≤n), and a prime number ppp. Assign Ai×pA_i \times pAi​×p to AiA_iAi​.
Pick an index iii (1≤i≤n1 \le i \le n1≤i≤n), and a prime number ppp such that AiA_iAi​ is divisible by ppp. Assign Ai÷pA_i \div pAi​÷p to aia_iai​.
The goal is to make all the numbers in array equal using the minimum number of operations. Among all sequences of operations using the minimum number of operations, denote SSS to be the **smallest** final value of all elements in AAA. You are given QQQ queries of the form (Li,Ri)(L_{i}, R_{i})(Li​,Ri​). For each query, you need to calculate the SSS value of the contiguous subarray ALi…RiA_{L_i \ldots R_i}ALi​…Ri​​.


Input Format

The first line contains NNN and QQQ denoting the number of elements in the array and number of queries.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​ denoting the given array.
QQQ lines follow. The iii-th line contains two space-separated integers LiL_{i}Li​ and RiR_{i}Ri​ denoting the iii-th query.


Output Format
For each query, output the value SSS for the subarray on a single line.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases ≤ 10^6
Sum of Q over all test cases ≤ 2·10^5
content.inputFormat:
The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case:
- The first line contains two integers N and Q — the size of the array and the number of queries.
- The second line contains N space‑separated positive integers A1 … AN (1 ≤ Ai ≤ 10^6).
- Each of the next Q lines contains two integers Li and Ri (1 ≤ Li ≤ Ri ≤ N) describing a query.
All indices are 1‑based.
content.outputFormat: For each query, output a single line containing the smallest possible final value S after performing the minimum number of operations on the subarray A[Li…Ri]. The value can be large; print it as a decimal integer without leading zeros.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
For the first query, there is only one sequence of operations that uses 222 operations:

Choose i=2i = 2i=2 and p=11p = 11p=11, assign A2×11=77A_2 \times 11 = 77A2​×11=77 to A2A_2A2​.
Choose i=3i = 3i=3 and p=7p = 7p=7, assign A3×7=77A_3 \times 7 = 77A3​×7=77 to A3A_3A3​.



We can prove that there aren't any sequence of operations that uses 111 operation, so the above sequence uses the least number of operations. The final SSS value is 777777.


For the second query, there are two sequences of operations that uses 222 operations. Those are:

Choose i=3i = 3i=3 and p=7p = 7p=7, assign A3×7=77A_3 \times 7 = 77A3​×7=77 to A3A_3A3​.
Choose i=4i = 4i=4 and p=7p = 7p=7, assign A4÷7=11A_4 \div 7 = 11A4​÷7=11 to A4A_4A4​.



Both of them are optimal in terms of number of operations. The first sequence of operations has the final value SSS to be 777777, and the second sequence of operations has the final value SSS to be 111111. Therefore, the answer to this query is 111111 (we need to minimize the final value SSS).

solution.approach:
For each array element we store its prime factorisation as a list of (prime, exponent). For a fixed prime p the exponent of p in an element is either 0 (p does not divide the element) or a small integer (≤19 because 2^19 ≤ 10^6). In a subarray the optimal final exponent of p is the **lower median** of all exponents of p inside the subarray – this minimises the total number of +/-1 operations and, among all optimal targets, yields the smallest final value. Therefore the answer for a query is   S = ∏ p^{median_p} .

The problem becomes: for many sub‑array queries, maintain for every prime p the multiset of its exponents in the current range and be able to obtain the lower median quickly. Because the exponent range is tiny, we keep for each prime p an array cnt[p][e] (e = 1 … maxExp(p)) that stores how many numbers in the current range have exponent exactly e. The number of zeros is   zeroCnt = rangeLength – Σ_{e>0} cnt[p][e].   To obtain the lower median we scan the exponents from 0 upwards, accumulating counts until we reach (rangeLength+1)/2. The scan touches at most 20 values, i.e. O(1).

We answer the queries offline with Mo's algorithm. While moving the current range left/right we add or remove a single array element. Adding/removing an element touches only the few primes that appear in this element (≤7). For each affected prime we update its cnt array (O(1)) and recompute its median by the O(20) scan described above. When a median changes we update the global product S accordingly (multiply or divide by p^{oldMedian} and by p^{newMedian}). The product can be huge, therefore we store it in a big‑integer type (Python int, C++ boost::multiprecision::cpp_int, Java BigInteger, JavaScript BigInt).

Overall complexity:
- Pre‑processing factorisation of all Ai in O(N log log MAX_A) using a linear sieve.
- Mo's algorithm performs O((N+Q)·√N) add/remove operations. Each operation touches ≤7 primes and each prime needs ≤20 steps, so the total time is roughly O((N+Q)·√N·140) which fits the limits.
- Memory: we keep for every prime that ever appears a small vector of size (maxExp+1) (≤20). The number of distinct primes ≤ 78498, so memory is well below 100 MB.

The algorithm works for the full constraints and also for the tiny test cases required for the sample section.
solution.timeComplexity: O((N + Q) · √N · Pmax) where Pmax ≤ 20 (maximum exponent). In practice ≈ O((N+Q)·√N).
